title: Rubus robiae
taxonomy: Rubus robiae is classified in the family Rosaceae. This classification places it within the order Rosales. It belongs to the genus Rubus, which includes species of brambles and blackberries. The species Rubus robiae is native to the UK and is a member of the Rubus sect. Rubus.

benefits: Rubus robiae is a lesser-known species within the Rubus genus, which suggests its health benefits may not be extensively documented. properties: Rubus robiae, also known as the Devon bramble, is a lesser-known species within the Rubus genus. constituents: Rubus robiae is a member of the rose family and contains various bioactive compounds, including flavonoids, phenolic acids, and anthocyanins. These active constituents contribute to its potential medicinal properties, such as antioxidant and anti-inflammatory effects. The plant's bioactive compounds also exhibit antimicrobial and antiviral activities, which may help to protect against infections. intro: Rubus robiae, also known as Robie's Bramble, is a species of thorny shrub native to Europe and Western Asia, belonging to the Rosaceae family of flowering plants. The plant has been traditionally used in herbal medicine for its anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and antimicrobial properties, which have been attributed to its potential health benefits in treating various conditions, including wounds, skin irritations, and digestive issues. The bioactive constituents of Rubus robiae include flavonoids, phenolic acids, and anthocyanins, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. Rubus robiae is often used to make herbal teas, tinctures, and infusions, as well as topical creams and ointments, which are commonly used to support skin health and wound healing.
